mentions.note: Good speculation despite the failed breakout; Cramer would add to the position rather than sell the rest.
Cramer flagged the $2B bond offering as 'shaky' and part of the debt deluge weighing on market liquidity.
Gigantic private fundraising adds to capital drain; Cramer says it had better come public soon given endless cash needs.
A huge disappointment that Cramer exited; chart looks decent but the story simply isn't working — stay away.

Precision aerospace and power generation components supplier with 26% Q1 growth; high barriers to entry; valuation at 55x EV/EBITDA vs. HII at 46x; wait for $42 (HII valuation equivalent) or $30s for discount entry.

Down 26% from $194 peak to $143; typical post-spin weakness (similar to Solstice recovery); largest LTL provider in attractive freight market; independent management can focus on service and growth; buying opportunity.
Down 10% from its high on a misread quarter — margin drag was zero-margin fuel surcharges and guidance confusion from the fiscal-year change; under 18x a conservative forecast.
Great buy right here after being crushed; remains the best name in turbines, which are in very short supply.
Its $45B slug within an $85B offering priced well for participants, but chasers were hurt; cited as evidence of supply soaking up sideline cash.
The tried-and-true comparable at a cheaper enterprise multiple (46 vs TPC's 55); Cramer likes the stock very much.
Beat, raised full-year guidance and hiked the dividend 14%; women's, tops, Beyond Yoga and 51% DTC mix give a 170-year-old brand real runway.
Inexpensive at six times earnings with a 5% yield and should bounce, but it's a China-dependent commodity name — sell into any strength.
Even McDonald's is getting hit — the whole fast-food group is very challenged.
Referenced as SK Hynix's U.S. competitor in the red-hot DRAM memory shortage; no direct call.
One of the cheapest S&P names versus its growth rate after shedding $1T in market cap; own it, don't trade it — and not because of China headlines.
Massive private fundraising competes for the same capital pool; Cramer wants it public given its cash requirements.
Big debt deals from Oracle add to the supply overhang Cramer is worried about.
Raised $1.2B selling 75M shares at $15.50, far below the $20 print — a deep-in-the-hole deal that signals supply strain.
Cheap if the data center trade holds, but a $29B raise with no retail appeal would force institutions to sell other stocks to fund it.
Cited as a post-spin-off template: fell from $53 to $40 before rallying to $90; Element Solutions deal was a good one despite market skepticism.

Morgan Stanley's Adam Jonas initiates at Overweight with a $300 target driven by Starlink connectivity and orbital compute, with space only 3% of the base case; buy some here and more lower for the long haul.

Morgan Stanley analyst Jonas initiated Overweight with $300 PT; thesis: Starlink enterprise demand (2.2B robots by 2040), orbital compute data centers, terrestrial revenues; space is only 3% of value; buy some now, more on pullbacks if execution credible.
$85B IPO plus $2B of bonds sopped up an enormous amount of capital; participants profited but chasers did not.
Caller with inherited shares told to sell: no growth, and Cramer won't own a stock just for yield — especially one competing with Starlink.
Used only as a post-spin-off precedent (fell $45 to $27, back to $36); Cramer cannot opine on his parent company's spin-off.
Digital mix and AI drive-throughs won't outrun the U.S. consumer slump; franchisee base too weak for the turnaround — no to owning it.
